topic: Zaha Hadid documentary — 'she made them take her seriously'

turns.content: I just watched that Zaha Hadid documentary and I am EMOTIONAL
Which one was it? There are a few about her. What hit you the hardest?

the one about her early career, before any of her buildings got built. like decades of winning competitions and then the projects getting cancelled or taken away. the Cardiff Bay Opera House thing where she won the competition fair and square and they just... gave it to someone else. I was yelling at my screen wallah

The Cardiff Bay story is one of the most frustrating episodes in modern architecture. She won in 1994 and a combination of political pressure, media hostility, and what many believe was racism and sexism led to the project being taken from her. The fact that she didn't quit architecture after that is extraordinary.

that's exactly what got me!! she'd been practicing for like 15 years at that point and STILL hadn't built a major building. everyone called her a paper architect. and she was just like no, I'm right and you're wrong and eventually you'll see. and she was an Arab woman in London in the 80s doing this!! the documentary showed how the British press talked about her and ngl it was disgusting. they called her designs aggressive and impossible. but when men did deconstructivism it was visionary

The double standard was glaring. Gehry, Libeskind, Eisenman -- all pushing radical forms at the same time and celebrated as mavericks. Zaha was doing the same work and characterized as difficult and impractical. "Aggressive" is not a word critics used for Gehry's Bilbao. The gendered and racialized language in how her work was received versus her male peers is well documented.

EXACTLY. Gehry literally wraps buildings in crumpled metal and he's a genius but Zaha draws a flowing curve and she's unrealistic. tbh watching this made me feel so many things at once. inspired because she actually made them take her seriously. but also angry because she had to fight ten times harder to get there. and I mean... the parallels aren't lost on me. obviously on a completely different scale. but the feeling of not being taken seriously, of having your work dismissed or taken by someone else? that I understand

You don't have to be at her scale for the parallels to be real. The structural barriers she faced -- being underestimated, having work attributed to others, being held to a different standard -- operate at every level. The fact that you recognize those patterns at your stage means you're already more aware than most people ever become.

the part that really made me cry was near the end. after the Pritzker Prize, they showed her walking through her completed buildings. and she didn't even look triumphant. she just looked... relieved. like finally. and then she died at 65 with so much more she could have done. it's not fair wallah

It's deeply unfair. She spent the first half of her career fighting to build anything and the second half building everything at once, as if making up for lost time. When she died in 2016 she had projects on every continent and had changed what people thought architecture could look like. But you're right that the expression in those later photos isn't joy -- it's vindication after decades of being told she was wrong.

I think what I'm taking away is that I need to stop waiting for permission. she never waited for anyone to tell her she belonged. she just kept designing and kept showing up. I need that energy. even when I feel like an impostor. just keep going

That's the most important lesson from her career. She didn't wait to be invited -- she made her own space. The impostor feeling doesn't go away by waiting for external validation; it goes away by building a body of work you can't deny. Zaha's answer to every rejection was another design. Keep designing, keep showing up.
